coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon dots regulate porous hollow nickel-cobalt sulfide: High-performance electrode materials in supercapacitor and enzymeless glucose sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Zhiwei
Zhang, Yan
Sun, Mengmeng
Zou, Ping
Wang, Xianxiang
Wang, Yanying
Huang, Qianming
Chen, Huaping
Ye, Jianshan
Rao, Hanbing
coredata.dc:description: Transition metal sulfides (TMSs) are expected to be a new type of electrode material due to their excellent electrochemical activity. However, the poor capacity retention and stability of TMSs are obstacles to their practical application. Here, the porous hollow nickel-cobalt sulfides are fabricated via a simple water bath followed by anion exchange, which can be regulated by N-doped carbon dots (NCDs). Newly designed porous hollow structure provides more active sites, a cavity for storing electrolytes, and optimized electron/ion diffusion pathways. Then, the Ni-Co ratio and the NCDs doping mass are optimized, and the as-prepared NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 displays a specific capacity of 764.1 C g−1 at 1 A g−1. A hybrid supercapacitor based on NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 achieves an outstanding energy/power density (73 mWh cm−2/1.39 W cm−2) and exhibits favorable cyclic performance (96.5% maintain for 10 000 times). In addition, a glucose sensor based on NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) is designed, with a wide detection range of 1 × 10−5-5 × 10−3 M and a low detection limit of 3.8 × 10−7 M (S/N = 3). The excellent electrochemical performance demonstrates the effectiveness of the manufacturing strategy of adjusting the properties of hollow-structured metal sulfides through carbon dots and provides a novel way to design new nanocomposite materials used for the multi-functional field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ni2CoS4
Carbon dots
Porous hollow structure
Supercapacitor
Glucose sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of NCDs-doped Nickel Cobalt acetate hydroxide precursors (pre-NCDs/NixCo3-xS-y)
Preparation of N-P co-doped biomass-derived porous carbon (NPBC)
Electrochemical analysis for the supercapacitor performance
Electrochemical performance of the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC HSC device
Electrochemical property of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE for the glucose sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Two-Way Support Team Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Sichuan Province Science and Technology Support Program

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the grants from the Sichuan Science and Technology Program (Grant No. 2021YJ0316 and 2021YFG0250 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22104102 , 21875070 , and 21905186 ), and the Two-Way Support Team Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University (Grant Nos. 2021993036 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM graphics of pre-NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 and (b) NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50. (c) TEM and HR-TEM graphics of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50. (d) Scanning TEM image of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 and corresponding EDS element mapping images for Ni, Co, S, C, O, and N.
(a) Full-scan XPS spectra of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 and (b–f) the corresponding high-resolution XPS spectra of Ni 2p, Co 2p, S 2p, C 1s, N 1s, respectively.

Comparison of (a) CV curves, (b) GCD curves and (c) EIS plots about (i) Ni2Co1S, (ii) NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50, (iii) NCDs/Ni2Co1S-100 and (iv) NCDs/Ni2Co1S-150. (d) CV curves of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 at different scan rates, (e) GCD curves of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 at different current density. (f) Rate performance of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50.

(a) Schematic illustration of the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC HSC device, (b) CV curves of NPBC and NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 at their corresponding potential ranges. (c) CV curves and (d) GCD curves of the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC were performed at various operating voltages. (e) CV curves of the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC were performed at different scan rates. (f) GCD curves of the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC at various current densities, (g) Rate performance of the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC. (i) Cyclic performance and coulombic efficiencies of the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC and (h) the Nyquist plots measured before and after the cycling. (j) Ragone plots of the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC and previously reported supercapacitors based on NiCoS.

Photographic images of two series-connected NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC HSC devices powering a green LED (∼2.9V) in the light (a) and the dark (b). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) CV curves of (i) bare GCE, (iii) Ni2Co1S/GCE, (v) NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE measured in 0.1 M NaOH and (ii) bare GCE, (iv) Ni2Co1S/GCE, (vi) NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE measured in 0.1 M NaOH containing 1 mM glucose. (b) The magnified view of curves (i) and (ii). (c) CV curves of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE at various scan rates. (d) Plot of redox peak current vs. scan rates. (e) Amperometric responses of the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE to continuous injection of glucose, (f) the calibration curve of response current and glucose concentration.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of NCDs (a), NCDs/NiCoS nanocomposites (b), and the formation of the hollow structure (c).
Determination and recovery of glucose based on NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE in human urine (n = 5).

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon dots regulate porous hollow nickel-cobalt sulfide: High-performance electrode materials in supercapacitor and enzymeless glucose sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Xin Kang Road, Yucheng District, Ya'an, 625014, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Xin Kang Road, Yucheng District, Ya’an, 625014, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Fuel Cell Technology of Guangdong Province, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510641, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Key Laboratory of Fuel Cell Technology of Guangdong Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Fuel Cell Technology of Guangdong Province, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510641, P.R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors wish it to be known that, in their opinions, Zhiwei Lu and Yan Zhang should be regarded as joint First Authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Transition metal sulfides (TMSs) are expected to be a new type of electrode material due to their excellent electrochemical activity. However, the poor capacity retention and stability of TMSs are obstacles to their practical application. Here, the porous hollow nickel-cobalt sulfides are fabricated via a simple water bath followed by anion exchange, which can be regulated by N-doped carbon dots (NCDs). Newly designed porous hollow structure provides more active sites, a cavity for storing electrolytes, and optimized electron/ion diffusion pathways. Then, the Ni-Co ratio and the NCDs doping mass are optimized, and the as-prepared NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 displays a specific capacity of 764.1 C g−1 at 1 A g−1. A hybrid supercapacitor based on NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 achieves an outstanding energy/power density (73 mWh cm−2/1.39 W cm−2) and exhibits favorable cyclic performance (96.5% maintain for 10 000 times). In addition, a glucose sensor based on NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) is designed, with a wide detection range of 1 × 10−5-5 × 10−3 M and a low detection limit of 3.8 × 10−7 M (S/N = 3). The excellent electrochemical performance demonstrates the effectiveness of the manufacturing strategy of adjusting the properties of hollow-structured metal sulfides through carbon dots and provides a novel way to design new nanocomposite materials used for the multi-functional field.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapidly increasing demand for environmentally friendly energy and clinical diagnosis of diabetes accelerate further research on energy storage/conversion devices and biosensors [1,2]. Supercapacitors (SCs) have become vital and promising energy storage/conversion devices due to their environmental friendliness, extreme response rate, and exceptional service life. SCs could be divided into electrical double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) and pseudocapacitors. EDLCs rely on carbon-based materials with high specific surface area [3,4], while the pseudocapacitors focus on the reversible Faraday reaction [5]. Pseudocapacitance electrodes, e.g. conductive polymers and transition metal oxides/sulfides can provide higher capacitance and energy density. Among them, Multi-transition metal oxides/hydroxides/sulfides deliver outstanding redox capacity and enhanced electrocatalytic activity because of their mixed-valence state [6]. Bimetallic sulfides have been intensively researched in SCs, overall water splitting, and lithium/sodium ion storage due to their excellent electrochemical activity, such as nickel-cobalt sulfide (NiCoS) [7], copper-cobalt sulfide [8], nickel-iron sulfide [9] and cobalt-molybdenum sulfide [10]. Interestingly, metal chalcogenides not only can be used as precursors of these highly active metal oxides [11], but also bimetal-based sulfides multivalent elements (i.e. Ni3+/Ni2+, Fe3+/Fe2+, and Co3+/Co2+) exhibit enhanced catalytic activity [12]. Moreover, the effective surface reconstruction process of bimetal-based sulfides remains a challenge in improving the electrocatalytic properties of materials [13]. In addition, according to previous studies, NiCoS is a suitable material to enhance Faraday pseudocapacitance and promote glucose oxidation [6,7]. On the other hand, many detection methods for glucose have been investigated, including electrochemiluminescence [14], fluorescence [15], spectrophotometry [16], and electrochemical methods [17]. Because of the fast response, high sensitivity, and stability, the electrochemical method is considered to be a promising method. The detection methods relied on glucose oxidase have been widely researched on account of the high sensitivity and specificity, but the complex fixation methods, unstable activity, and high cost limit the application [17]. Consequently, it's critical to developing a low-cost enzyme-free glucose sensor.

Compared with single metal sulfides (such as NiS [18] and Co9S8 [19]), NiCoS possesses higher conductivity and specific capacitance [6]. However, NiCoS tends to aggregate during preparation and operation, resulting in low electrode material utilization and poor cyclic stability. Controllable morphologies of NiCoS play critical roles in their performance. Currently, NiCoS with different morphologies have been prepared by controlling the synthesis conditions and utilizing the morphology of the precursor/growth substrate. Zhang et al. [20] prepared various morphologies of NiCo2S4 based on two-step hydrothermal method via different solvents, the tube-like NiCo2S4 delivered a specific capacitance of 1048 F g−1 at 3 A g−1. Guan et al. [21] prepared multilayer core-shell NiCo2S4 particles through onion-like Co3O4 particles, with a specific capacitance of 1016 F g−1 at 2 A g−1. Constructing a reasonable structure or employing graphene oxide (GO) [22], carbon nanotube (CNT), and activated carbon (AC) [23] to boost the surface area is conducive to increase capacitance and improve cycle performance. Cao et al. [24] reported 10–15 nm ultra-small CoNi2S4 nanoparticles anchored on CNT sponge by electrodeposition, provided specific capacitance of 1530 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. However, introducing inherently low-capacitance carbon materials would reduce the overall specific capacitance [6]. Moreover, the preparation of carbon nanomaterials (such as GO and CNT) is complex and expensive, which increases the cost of preparation.

On account of the unique physical, chemical properties, and low cost, carbon dots (CDs) have been investigated in many fields, such as SCs [25], electrocatalysis [26], photocatalysis [27], and biosensing [28]. The introduction of CDs can improve the conductivity of composite materials and accelerate surface charge transfer [25,29]. In addition, urchin-like Co(OH)2 regulated by NCDs has been investigated as an efficient OER catalyst [26]. The regulating effect of CDs on composite materials was attributed to the attraction of negatively charged CDs to metal ions, then controlled crystal growth [30,31]. CDs, which are rich in surface functional groups and small in size, promote the wetting of the electrode surface and stabilize the surface state to improve the specific capacitance and cycle stability [25,32,33]. Based on the above considerations, anchoring CDs into NiCoS could be an effective strategy for preparing composite materials with excellent electrochemical performance.

In this work, the NCDs incorporated NiCoS electrode materials (NCDs/NiCoS) with porous hollow structures were designed and prepared by the combination of the water bath and hydrothermal reaction, and successfully employed for supercapacitors and electrochemical glucose sensors. The excellent performance of NCD/NiCoS in supercapacitors and glucose detection was attributed to the morphology adjustment of NiCoS by NCDs and the porous hollow structure caused by anion exchange. The prepared NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 electrode delivered specific capacity of 764.1 C g−1 at 1 A g−1 and capacity retention of 71.7% at 20 A g−1. A hybrid supercapacitor (HSC) assembled by NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 and biomass-derived carbon have achieved high energy densities of 73 mWh cm−2/1.39 W cm−2 and 59.6 mWh cm−2/31.6 W cm−2. Besides, the capacitor remained 96.5% after 10 000 cycles. Meanwhile, the glucose sensor based on NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 shows a superior detection range and detection limit.

NCDs are synthesized from Broussonetia papyrifera through a simple hydrothermal route [34]. Broussonetia papyrifera branches were collected from the farm of Sichuan Agricultural University in Ya'an, China. The collected Broussonetia papyrifera branches were peeled and washed with Ultrapure water repeatedly. Afterward, the branches were then ground into powder by a pulverizer after dried at 80 °C overnight. And then, 2.5 g branches powder was added into a beaker with 50 mL DI water solution and stirred for 5 min. After that, the suspension was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling, the brown NCDs aqueous solution was collected after vacuum filtration. The solution was dried with a vacuum rotary evaporator at 60 °C to obtain NCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NCDs-doped Nickel Cobalt acetate hydroxide precursors (pre-NCDs/NixCo3-xS-y)
ce:section.ce:para: According to the reported literature [35], the NCDs-doped Nickel Cobalt acetate hydroxide precursors were prepared by a simple reaction. NCDs were added to 200 mL ethanol with vigorous stirring for 10 min. After that, 3.0 g Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and 1.28 g mixed metal acetate tetrahydrate were dissolved into the mixed solution at room temperature. And then, the solution was heated to 85 °C under reflux conditions. The solid was separated by centrifugation after reaction for 4 h, and then washed with ethanol 6 times to remove PVP. The product was dried in a vacuum at 60 °C overnight. Precursors with different NCDs contents at different metal ratios were obtained via changing the weight of NCDs and the molar ratio of Ni-Co. The final product is denoted as pre-NCDs/NixCo3-xS-y (x = 1, 1.5, 2, where y represents the mass (mg) of NPCs added in the reaction. When y is 0, y and NCDs are omitted).

For the formation of NCDs/NixCo3-xS-y nanocomposites, 80 mg of the above pre-NCDs/NixCo3-xS-y was re-dispersed into 40 mL ethanol with vigorous stirring. Subsequently, 0.1125 g thioacetamide (TAA) was added to the suspension. After stirring for 10 min, the suspension was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. After heated at 120 °C for 6 h, the NCDs/NixCo3-xS-y nanocomposites were obtained after vacuum filtration, washing, and vacuum drying (60 °C for 12 h).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-P co-doped biomass-derived porous carbon (NPBC)
ce:section.ce:para: NPBC was prepared by the one-step carbonization activation method [36], In the first step, 10 g above Broussonetia papyrifera powder was impregnated in an aqueous solution containing 30g (NH3)2HPO4 for 4 h and then freeze-dried. The as-obtained dry solids were put into a tube furnace under the air atmosphere at 200 °C for 2 h. After that, the atmosphere was changed to Ar, and the furnace was heated to 800 °C. After reaction for 2 h, the furnace naturally cooled to room temperature. All heating processes are at a rate of 5 °C min−1. After natural cooling, the solid was immersed in HCl solution (1 M) overnight, and then NPBC was obtained after vacuum filtration, washing, and vacuum drying (60 °C for 12 h).

The electrochemical properties of the electrodes were performed on a CS electrochemical workstation (Wuhan Corrtest, China) in 1 M KOH, i.e., electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and cyclic voltammetry (CV). In a three-electrode system, Pt foil acted as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode acted as the reference electrode. The working electrodes was prepared by a coating method. The sample, carbon black, and polytetrafluoroethylene binder (7:2:1) with 200 μL 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone were ground to a homogenate, and then coated on the Nickel foam current collector (1.5 × 1.5 cm2, ≈60 mg) followed vacuum dried at 120 °C overnight. The dry electrode was pressed under 10 MPa for 2 min. Before used, the Nickel foam was cleaned with ultrasonic in acetone, 0.1 M hydrochloric acid, and ethanol for 5 min, respectively. The mass loading of the electroactive material on the Nickel foam was approximately 2 mg.

A hybrid supercapacitor (HSC) was assembled with NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 as the battery-type electrode (+ve), NPBC as the capacitive-type electrode (-ve), and PVA-KOH gel as electrolyte. According to the charge balance, the mass loading ratio between + ve and -ve electrodes is 0.29. The NPBC electrode was prepared by the above method of preparing the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 electrode. PVA-KOH gel electrolyte was prepared by adding 6 g KOH and 6 g polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to 80 mL of DI water and stirring continuously at 85 °C until a homogeneous solution was formed [37,38]. Then, the positive electrode and the negative electrode were immersed in the above-mentioned hot electrolyte for 0.5 h to ensure that the electrolyte completely penetrated the electrode. Subsequently, the electrodes were dried at 80 °C overnight. Finally, the HSC device was assembled by inserting a cellulose separator between the two poles and encapsulated with aluminum plastic film.

The chronoamperometry was performed to detect glucose, by continuously adding glucose to 0.1 M NaOH under constant stirring. In a three-electrode system Pt wire acted as the counter electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode acted as the reference electrode and glassy carbon electrode (GCE, Φ = 3 mm) acted as the working electrode. 5 mg of active materials were dispersed in 1 mL 0.2% chitosan-acetic acid solution by ultrasound, and 10 μL of the mixed suspension was dropped on the glassy carbon surface of the GCE and was dried under 60 °C. Before that, GCE was polished with aluminum powder and then cleaned with ultrasonic. Furthermore, urine samples were obtained from the school hospital (Sichuan Agricultural University, Ya’An, China). Urine samples were diluted 1000 times with 0.1 M NaOH and quantified by adding a known concentration of glucose solution into blank urine with standard addition methods. All electrochemical tests were performed at ambient condition.

The preparation process of the porous hollow NCDs/NiCoS was schematically presented in Scheme 1 (a-b). Considering its abundant heteroatoms and low cost [39,40], Broussonetia papyrifera was chosen as the raw material for the preparation of NCDs and porous carbon cathode. Then, with the assistance of PVP, NCDs-doped Ni-Co acetate hydroxide nano prisms were synthesized as precursors by the water bath method. The reaction during the synthesis of the pre-NCDs/NixCo3-xS-y can be described by the formula [41]:
(1)

H 2
O + 2 C
H 3
C O O H

Finally, TAA was used to hydrothermally sulfide the precursor to obtain NCDs-embedded porous hollow NiCoS. The reactions could be described by the following formulas [35,42]:
(2)

Anion exchange based on the Kirkendall effect was the key to the formation of a hollow structure [43] (Scheme 1(c)). The S2− produced by the hydrolysis of the TAA reacts with the metal ions on the precursor surface to form a thin metal sulfide layer at the beginning of the reaction. The sulfide layer hinders the subsequent direct chemical reaction between S2− and internal metal ions. The continuation of the reaction depends on the ions' diffusion. The outward-diffusion speed of metal ions is faster than the inward-diffusion speed of S2−. The metal sulfide formed by the subsequent reaction accumulates on the initially formed metal sulfide film, then a shell structure composed of metal sulfide formed, gradually. NCDs were uniformly doped into the NiCoS shell during the sulfidation. Subsequently, the hollow shell structure was formed due to the exhaustion of the precursor [44,45]. In addition, the static high pressure generated during the hydrothermal reaction partially brokes the hollow shell structure [46]. Finally, NCDs-doped porous hollow NiCoS with rapid electron transfer charge channels and improved electrochemical activity were synthesized.

The morphology of pre-NCDs/Ni2Co1S-y (y represents the mass (mg) of NPCs added) and NCDs/Ni2Co1S-y were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The SEM images of the pre-NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 were presented in Fig. 1 (a), which shows a highly homogeneous rounded nanoprism-like structure. The pre-NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 particles are around 140 mm long and 80 nm wide. Compared with pre-Ni2Co1S (Fig. S1(a)), the volume of pre-NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 is approximatively one-eleventh. With the increased quantity of NCDs, the nanoprism-like morphology of the precursors (pre-NCDs/Ni2Co1S-100 and pre-NCDs/Ni2Co1S-150) gradually turned into a lumpy shape (Fig. S1(b-c)). Fig. 1(b) shows the SEM images of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50. As mentioned above, the hollow shell structure is inherited from the precursor, and the shell structure is squeezed and partially broken under the action of hydrothermal pressure. Similarly, the same phenomenon was observed in the SEM image of Ni2Co1S (Fig. S1(d)). The structure of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-100 and NCDs/Ni2Co1S-150 are similar to the precursors and present a corresponding lumpy shape (Fig. S1(e-f)). Generally, the block structure hinders the exposure of active sites. In addition, pre-NCDs/Ni1Co1S-50 (Fig. S2(a)) and pre-NCDs/Ni1Co2S-50 (Fig. S2(b)) show a similarly homogeneous rounded nanoprism-like structure. These products NCDs/Ni1Co1S-50 (Fig. S2(c)) and NCDs/Ni1Co2S-50 (Fig. S2(d)) show a similarly porous hollow shell structure. However, due to the change of Ni-Co ratio, the corresponding size has changed significantly. The hollow shell structure of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 provides more electrolyte contact surface, and the open structure is also conducive to the mass transport in the electrolyte during rapid charging and discharging, which was beneficial to supercapacitors [47]. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) measurement was performed for NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50. The estimated total BET-specific surface area for the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 sample was 52.1 cm2 g−1. Fig. S3(a) shows the type-H4 hysteresis loop and type-IV N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50, corresponding to the micropore/mesopore structure in the sample [48,49]. Meanwhile, the Barret-Joyner-Halenda results in Fig. S3(b) suggested that the existence of micropores and mesopores at the range of 1–5 nm in the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 sample. The micropores are mainly derived from anion exchange during the thiolation process [50,51] as well as the NCDs etching process. At the same time, the micropores increase the specific surface area of materials and improve the availability of materials. Besides, microporous adsorption also solves the problem that electrolyte is easy to dissolve and leads to capacity attenuation. Mesopores promote the electrolyte wetting of the physical surface of the sample to increase the electrochemically accessible active sites [47]. The above structure indicates the promising extraordinary capacitance capability of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50.

Fig. S4(a-b) exhibit the X-ray diffraction spectra (XRD) patterns of pre-NCDs/Ni2Co1S-y (Fig. S4a) and NCDs/Ni2Co1S-y (Fig. S4b), respectively. The sharp and consistent diffraction peaks indicate a good crystallinity of pre-NCDs/Ni2Co1S-y [41]. As shown in the XRD patterns of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-y, the diffraction peaks could be identified as Ni2CoS4 (JCPDS cards # 24–0334). Four representative peaks at 3.14°, 38.2°, 50.3°, and 55.0° belong to (311), (400), (511), and (440) planes of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-y. Furthermore, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) graphics suggest the uniform structure NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 (Fig. 1(c)). The fringes spacing of 0.28 and 0.23 nm in high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) graphics correspond to the (311) and (400) planes of Ni2CoS4, which correspond to the results of the XRD patterns [52,53]. More detailed HR-TEM images of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 are shown in Fig. S5. In addition, the TEM of NCDs shown that the diameter of the homogeneously dispersed NCDs is approximately 5 nm (Fig. S6(a)). The high definition lattice fringe suggested the high degree crystallinity of NCDs (Fig. S6(b)) [26,54]. TEM and EDS elemental mapping images (Fig. 1(d)) suggest the homogeneous existent of Co, Ni, S, C, O, and N elements of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50. The uniform scattering of C species (green) and N species (red) indicates the uniform incorporation of NCDs. Furthermore, the EDS spectra of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 were shown in Fig. S7, the elements including Ni, Co, S, C, O, and N can be clearly seen.

The chemical composition of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The overall survey scan spectrum of the sample contained Ni 2p, Co 2p, O 1s, C 1s, N 1s, and S 2p (Fig. 2 (a)), indicating that NCDs have been successfully incorporated into NiCoS. In the 2p region of Ni (Fig. 2(b)), the characteristic peaks at 853.5 eV and 870.8 eV corresponding to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 of Ni2+. The remaining peaks corresponding to Ni 2p3/2 (856.9 eV) and Ni 2p1/2 (874.9 eV) of Ni3+ and the spin splitting of the two satellite peaks (861.8 eV and 879.7 eV), respectively [55]. For Co 2p (Fig. 2(c)), the binding energies at 778.8 eV for Co 2p3/2 and 793.8 eV for Co 2p1/2 are characteristic peaks of Co2+, while the binding energies at 781.9 eV for Co 2p3/2 and 798.1 eV for Co 2p1/2 are assigned to Co3+, and two satellite peaks are observed [56]. The S 2p spectrum (Fig. 2(d)) is fitted to three peaks (162.1 eV, 163.4 eV and 168.9 eV) and one satellite peak, while the S4O6 2− could be the partial oxidation of the surface of NiCoS [24]. The XPS spectrum of C 1s in Fig. 2(e) exhibits four individual peaks at 284.3, 284.9, 285.8, and 287.1 eV [57,58]. The fitted N 1s spectra were assigned to pyrrolic-N (399.7 eV) and graphite-N (401.8 eV) (Fig. 2(f)) [59,60]. N doping can adjust the conduction band and valence band position of the doped carbon dots to improve the conductivity [61,62]. These results confirm the EDS results, suggest that a formation of Ni2Co1S4 doped with NCDs. Thus, the electrochemical performance of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 composites could be expected to be remarkably improved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical analysis for the supercapacitor performance
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 3(a) compares the CV curves of Ni2Co1S (i), NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 (ii), NCDs/Ni2Co1S-100 (iii), and NCDs/Ni2Co1S-150 (iv). All curves present obvious redox peaks, which indicate capacitive properties based on the reversible Faraday reaction. The redox reaction involved is based on the following formulas [63,64]:
(5)
N
i 2
C o
S 4
+ 2 O
H −
↔ C o
S
2 x
O H + 2 N
iS
2 -
x
O H + 2
e −
(6)
C o
S
2 x
O H + O
H −
↔ C o
S
2 x
O +
H 2
O +
e −

Obviously, compared with Ni2Co1S, the electrodes doped with NCDs exhibits higher oxidation current values, suggesting a higher electrochemical activity and energy storage capacity. The improvement in performances could be attributed to the regulation effect of NCDs on NiCoS. Furthermore, the optimal doped mass of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 compared with the electrodes doped with 100 mg and 150 mg NCDs (iii and iv), shows the most excellent redox performance. These results correspond with those of SEM results, this is due to the structural damage caused by excessive NCDs. The GCD curves of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-y were presented in Fig. 3 (b). All the GCD curves show a weak plateau rather than typical symmetrical linear triangles, which further confirms the Faraday redox behavior of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-y. Among the measured electrodes, NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 performs a longer charge/discharge time. NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 possessed a specific capacity of 764.1 C g−1, while Ni2Co1S, NCDs/Ni2Co1S-100 and NCDs/Ni2Co1S-150 are 538.6, 634 and 642.6 C g−1 at 1 A g−1. The performance of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 incurred by the excellent conductivity, which is confirmed via the EIS plots presented in Fig. 3(c). The charge transfer resistance of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 (Rct = 0.08 Ω) compared to Ni2Co1S (Rct = 0.33 Ω) indicated that the incorporation of NCDs can improve the conductivity of the composites. As the content of NCDs increases, the lines corresponding to the low-frequency region become more vertical, indicating that more NCDs are beneficial to the diffusion of electrolyte ions to the electrode surface [65,66]. The oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of NCDs facilitate the contact between electrodes and electrolyte ions, thereby increasing electrode utilization and increasing capacitance [25]. However, as the content of NCDs increases, the structures of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-100 and NCDs/Ni2Co1S-150 are destroyed, and the charge transfer resistance gradually increases. The NCDs/Ni2Co1s-50 of the oxygen-rich vacancy is preferred to form Co-OOH· species because the oxygen vacancy accelerates the adsorption of OH− ions, that is, accelerates the charge transfer and improves the deprotonation ability [13]. Besides, the same trend of CV and GCD curves results were observed at electrodes measurement with various Ni-Co ratios, such as NCDs/Ni1Co1S-y and NCDs/Ni1Co2S-y. As The CV curves comparison and GCD curves comparison plots shown in Fig. S9(a-b), NCDs/Ni1Co1S-50 shows a larger CV curve enclosed area, stronger peak current values, and longer charge/discharge time than Ni1Co1S, NCDs/Ni1Co1S-100, and NCDs/Ni1Co1S-150, which indicates a better capacitor performance. The specific capacities of Ni1Co1S, NCDs/Ni1Co1S-50, NCDs/Ni1Co1S-100 and NCDs/Ni1Co1S-150 are 393.4, 599.9, 533.3 and 493.2 C g−1 at 1 A g−1. Similarly, NCDs/Ni1Co2S-50 shows the best capacity performance (Fig. S10(a-b)). The specific capacities of Ni1Co2S, NCDs/Ni1Co2S-50, NCDs/Ni1Co2S-100 and NCDs/Ni1Co2S-150 328.4, 566.1, 410.8 and 468.9 C g−1 at 1 A g−1. The specific capacity values of the total 12 kinds of electrodes are shown in Fig. S11. In addition, as shown in the CV and GCD plots in Fig. S12(a-b), the composites with the Ni-Co ratio of 2:1 hold better electrochemical activity and larger specific capacity among the congeners with the same NCDs doping mass. Among the various electrodes, NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 possessed the highest specific capacity, which is the result of the synergistic effect between Ni-Co ratio and NCDs doping.

The CV curves at various scan rates of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 are presented in (Fig. 3(d)). The symmetrical CV curves at high scanning speeds, indicating good electrochemical reversibility of the electrode. The rate capability of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 was further investigated at various current densities (Fig. 3(e)). The almost equal charging and discharging time of each curve indicates the good coulombic efficiency of the electrode. Fig. 3(f) shows the specific capacity of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 at various current densities. Encouragingly, NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 shows an outstanding rate performance of at 20 A g−1 (71.7%). While the Ni2Co1S is only 150.9 C g−1 (28%) (Fig. S13). For NCDs/Ni2Co1S-100 and NCDs/Ni2Co1S-150 which are also doped with carbon dots, the rate performances at 20 A g−1 are 63.6% and 60.9%, respectively. A consistent phenomenon was observed in the GCD curves of NCDs/Ni1Co1S-y species (Fig. S9(c-d)) and NCDs/Ni1Co2S-y species (Fig. S10(c-d)) in this work. Certainly, the improved performance is due to the adjustment of NiCoS by NCDs. Noteworthy, the electrochemical performances of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 are comparable to the previously reported NiCoS materials (Table S1). In addition, the cycle stability of the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 electrode is shown in Fig. S14, the capacity maintained 84.6% after 4500 cycles of GCD measurement at 20 A g−1. Advantages of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 are summarized as follows. Firstly, the hollow shell structure exposes more Faraday redox-active sites. Secondly, the open structure shorts the ions' transmission path to satisfy the rapid charge-discharge. Finally, the incorporation of NCDs optimizes the morphology of the hollow shell structure, reduces the internal resistance, and further improves electrochemical performance. In addition, the transformations of Ni2+ and Co3+ on the catalyst surface after cycles 10 time was confirmed by ex-situ XPS (Fig. S15 and Fig. S16) and the related discussions in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC HSC device
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the excellent performance, NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 was used as the positive electrode (+Ve) to assemble the HSC devices. N-P co-doped biomass porous carbon (NPBC) was used as the negative electrode (-Ve) (Fig. 4 (a)). As shown in the CV and GCD curves in Fig. S17(a-b), The CV curve remains rectangular even at a large scan rate (100 mV s−1). The specific capacitance of NPBC at a current density of 1 A g−1 is 223.6 C g−1, and still maintains 73% of the initial capacity at 20 A g−1, which suggests that Broussonetia papyrifera derived NPBC was a promising negative electrode material. The CV curves of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 electrode and NPGC electrode presented in Fig. 4(b), which suggested that the combination of the two electrodes could expand the voltage of the HSC to 1.6 V. The CV curves at an operating voltage of 1.2–1.7 V of the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC were presented in Fig. 4(c). A slight polarization of the CV curve was observed for 1.7 V, which suggested that 0–1.6 V is more appropriate for the normal operation of the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC. Therefore, the GCD curves of the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC were measured under the operating voltage of 1.2–1.6 V. The GCD curves expanded proportionally with the working voltage increasing without deformation, suggests that both electrodes were effectively utilized (Fig. 4(d)). The CV curves at various scan rates were further performed at 0–1.6 V. Each curve shows the hybrid electrochemical behavior and maintains a consistent shape with the scan rate increase (Fig. 4(e)). The GCD curves were performed at 1–20 A g−1 to further evaluate the rate performance of the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC. As shown in Fig. 4(f), the charge times approximately equal to discharge times illustrate the outstanding energy conversion efficiency of the HSC. Fig. 4(g) shows the calculated capacity of the HSC, which still maintains 71.6% at 20 A g−1, suggesting an outstanding rate capability. The energy density of the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC is further calculated in Ragone plots in Fig. 4(j). The HSC possesses an energy/power density of 73 mWh cm−2/1.39 W cm−2 and keeps an energy/power density of 59.6 mWh cm−2/31.6 W cm−2. The as-fabricated NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC HSC device has better performance than formerly reported NiCoS-based devices (Table S2) [67–73]. The cyclic stability was presented in Fig. 4(i). Even after 10 000 cycles, the capacity remained at 96.5% of the initial value. Moreover, the coulombic efficiency was as high as 96% during the entire test process, stably. Furthermore, EIS analyses were performed for the HSC before and after the cycle. By fitting the Nyquist curves (Fig. 4(h)), the low charge transfer resistance before (Rct = 0.36 Ω) and after (Rct = 1.08 Ω) the cycle indicates the good conductivity of the device. The NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC was further used to power electronic equipment to verify its potential application prospects. As shown in Fig. 5 , two series-connected HSC devices powered a green light-emitting diode (LED, ∼2.9 V) to emit bright light. Overall, the as-fabricated NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50//NPBC HSC device shows promising application prospects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical property of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE for the glucose sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The performance of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 in catalyzing glucose oxidation was measured by CV (0–0.8 V). The enhanced current response of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE (v) compared with bare GCE (i) and Ni2Co1S/GCE (iii) on account of the outstanding conductivity and electrocatalytic activity of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 (Fig. 6 (a)). With the addition of glucose (1 mM), NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE (vi) exhibits a stronger peak oxidation current response than Ni2Co1S/GCE (iv), indicating a better electrocatalytic ability and conductivity of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50, which suggest that the incorporation of carbon dots is important. As shown in the enlarged CV curves (i and ii) in Fig. 6(b), only background current was detected, due to the lack of electrochemical activity of GCE. The catalytic oxidation of glucose by NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 could be attributed to the following mechanism. Firstly, during the anode scanning process, the Ni2+ and Co3+ species in NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 were oxidized into Ni3+ and Co4+ species in an alkaline solution, as Eq 5 and 6 illustrated above. Subsequently, Ni3+ and Co4+ catalyze the oxidation of glucose, as shown in the following formulas [17]:
(7)
N
i
3 +
+ glucose → N
i
2 +
+ gluconolactone
(8)
C
o
4 +
+ glucose → C
o
3 +
+ gluconolactone

Moreover, With the addition of glucose, glucose and oxidized intermediates are adsorbed on the electrode surface, and the oxidation peak potentials were slightly shifted to the higher potential [74]. Fig. 6(c) presents the CV curves of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE at various scan rates. Obviously, a positive correlation between peak currents and scan rates, and the redox potentials shift slightly. As shown in Fig. 6(d), the high linear correlation index suggests a surface-controlled glucose electrochemical oxidation process [75,76].

The chronoamperometry was performed to detect glucose. First, the active material loading mass of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE was optimized at 0.55 V. Fig. S18(a) presents the amperometric response of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE with various loading mass of 6–14 μL. A larger steady-state current response can be observed from the amperometric response curve with a load mass of 10 μL. It could be the result that the lower active material loading mass lacks sufficient active sites, while the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 film too thick may weaken the electrical conductivity [77]. Then, a chronoamperometric potential optimization experiment was further performed (Fig. S18(b)). The amperometric responses of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE were recorded at various applied potentials. Apparently, the amperometric response is stronger at 0.55 V, which is consistent with the oxidation peak potential. As shown in Fig. 6(e), the reliable applicability of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE in glucose sensing was analyzed by the chronoamperometry under the above-optimized conditions. The response current rose in steps with continuous injection of glucose at an interval of 30 s. Remarkably, the steady-state current reached the maximum value within 1 s after 100 μM glucose injection, indicating the rapid electron transfer kinetics of the sensor (Fig. S19). As shown in Fig. 6(f), in the range of 10–5000 μM, the calibration linear regression equation of current response and glucose concentration is:
I = 68.31 + 0.5053 ×
C
g l u
with
R 2
= 0.9936
, where ‘I’ is response current (μA), ‘C
glu ’ is glucose concentration (μM). The calculated detection limit (LOD) is 0.38 μM (S/N = 3), and the sensitivity is 7.15 μA μM−1 cm−2. The as-fabricated NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE sensor is competitive with previously reported enzymeless electrochemical glucose sensors (Table S3).

Selectivity is one of the important parameters of glucose sensors. Some possible interferences were selected to investigate the anti-interference of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE sensor performance, e.g. mannose, fructose, galactose, maltose, trehalose, lactose, dopamine (DA), ascorbic acid (AA), and Na+. The anti-interference of the sensor was measured by CV at 0–0.8 V in twice the concentration of interference. As shown in Fig. S20, compared with the oxidation peak current of glucose, it shows slight current changes in the presence of various interferences, which indicates that the excellent selectivity of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE sensor towards glucose. The repeatability and reproducibility of the developed sensor were measured by chronoamperometry. The relative standard deviation (RSD) calculated from six repeated experiments on the same modified electrode is 1.92%, showing the excellent repeatability of the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE sensor (Fig. S21(a)). The excellent reproducibility was further confirmed by repeating the preparation of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE sensor 6 times, and the RSD is 1.06% (Fig. S21(b)). In addition, the stability of the sensor stored for 20 days in a sealed and dry environment was evaluated by chronoamperometry (interval of 2 days). The amperometric response of the sensor remained 93.00% of the initial value even after repeated wetting, drying, and measuring, indicating good stability (Fig. S22). The standard addition method was performed for the diluted human urine containing various glucose to check the reliability of the sensor. The relevant results were listed in Table 1 , the recovery rate is in the range of 94.90–106.04%, and the RSD is less than 4.69% (n = 5). These results indicate the applicability and application potential of the as-prepared NCD/Ni2Co1S-50/GCE sensor in real sample analysis.

In summary, this work designed a simple two-step method to prepare NCDs-regulated porous hollow nickel-cobalt sulfide nanocomposites with enhanced electrochemical activity, as the battery-type electrode of supercapacitor and enzyme-free glucose sensor modification materials. The synthesis process involves a facile water bath synthesis of uniform NCDs doped Ni-Co acetate nanoparticles and a subsequent anion exchange sulfidation process. Chemical and microstructure characterization revealed that the formation of porous hollow structure NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 after successful NCDs incorporation and anion exchange. Electrochemical analysis shows that the NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 electrode possessed excellent capacity performance (764.1C g−1 at 1 A g−1) and favorable retention rate (7.17% at 20 A g−1). Furthermore, the HSC devices based on NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 and NPBC delivered high energy/power density and outstanding cyclic stability (96.5%, 10 000 cycles). Simultaneously, the glucose sensor based on NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 modified GCE exhibits excellent electrochemical performance and practicality. This excellent performance of NCDs/Ni2Co1S-50 owing to the excellent conductivity of NCDs and its adjustment to the morphology of the composite material, reasonable Ni-Co ratio, and the hollow shell with more active sites. Therefore, the manufacturing strategy of controllable synthesis of NCDs-doped hollow structure transition metal sulfide presents important practical significance for the fields of energy conversion/storage and electrochemical sensing.

Zhiwei Lu: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision. Yan Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Mengmeng Sun: Supervision, Data curation. Ping Zou: Investigation, Supervision. Xianxiang Wang: Data curation. Yanying Wang: Funding acquisition. Qianming Huang: Investigation. Huaping Chen: Conceptualization. Jianshan Ye: Resources, Funding acquisition. Hanbing Rao: Resources, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
